maxwell.headline: Ghislaine Maxwell's attorney publicly appealed for presidential clemency in exchange for testimony, prompting White House denial of priority and Senate opposition resolution.
maxwell.summary: Maxwell's legal team indicated preparation of a commutation application and stated she would testify fully if granted clemency by President Trump, invoking the Fifth Amendment during a House Oversight Committee deposition otherwise[1][2][3][4]. The White House press secretary confirmed on February 10, 2026, that a pardon request is not a priority and has not been discussed with the president[4]. Senator Jacky Rosen announced a Senate resolution opposing any clemency for Maxwell, citing her conviction for sex trafficking minors[5]. No official clemency petition has been filed as of February 2026, and her prior appeals, including a Supreme Court denial in October 2025 and habeas corpus denial in January 2026, have been exhausted[1].

iran.headline: US and Israel continue massive airstrikes on Iran through March 7, 2026, killing Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and targeting nuclear sites and leadership; Iran retaliates with drone and missile strikes on US embassies and Gulf targets.
iran.summary: Joint US-Israeli airstrikes began on February 28, 2026, assassinating Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and striking over 1,700 Iranian military, nuclear, and government targets, including the Supreme National Security Council in Tehran on March 3.[1][2] Iran responded with drone and missile attacks on US embassies in Riyadh, Kuwait, and Dubai, closing them, and hitting oil tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, with IRGC threatening to block oil shipments.[1][2] On March 7, US and Israeli forces struck infrastructure in Tehran and Isfahan, while Iran announced a new supreme leader amid ongoing hostilities.[2][4] Iranian President Pezeshkian delegated powers to provincial governors for continuity.[1]

gulf_swf.headline: Saudi PIF unveils revised 2026-2030 strategy focusing on industry, AI, mining, and tourism while scaling back mega-projects.
gulf_swf.summary: Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund (PIF) announced a new five-year strategy for 2026-2030, shifting from visionary mega-projects to industrial execution in sectors like logistics, mining, advanced manufacturing, clean energy, AI, and religious tourism.[2][3][4][5][6] The strategy was previewed at a private sector forum in Riyadh, emphasizing private sector partnerships with over 190 investment opportunities offered valued at SAR 40 billion.[3] PIF also plans IPOs for up to eight subsidiaries in 2026, including ArcelorMittal Jubail, Sela, and Saudi Global Ports.[1] No new developments reported on Mubadala, MGX, ADIA, QIA, GlobalFoundries, or Affinity Partners.

oracle_ellison.headline: Oracle announced $45-50B equity and debt financing plan for 2026 to fund cloud infrastructure expansion amid debt scrutiny.
oracle_ellison.summary: Oracle revealed plans on February 1, 2026, to raise $45-50 billion in gross cash proceeds through a balanced mix of debt and equity, including a $20B at-the-market equity program and a one-time senior unsecured bond issuance early in 2026, to support Oracle Cloud Infrastructure growth for customers like OpenAI.[1][4] The announcement addressed concerns over Oracle's $100B debt load and AI data center buildout, with Bank of America noting it defused a key 2026 risk after pricing $25B in bonds.[2] A proposed class action lawsuit from bondholders alleges Oracle failed to disclose debt needs tied to its OpenAI deal, filed last month.[3] No updates on Stargate contraction, Ellison WBD acquisition, or Paramount-Skydance mergers.

epstein_files.headline: DOJ continues removing and reviewing Epstein files amid criticism, with site last updated March 7, 2026.
epstein_files.summary: The DOJ has removed over 47,000 files (65,500 pages) from its public Epstein files release due to concerns over survivor information and explicit content, reducing the available documents to about 2.7 million pages as of late February 2026[1]. Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche stated the DOJ reviewed 6 million total pages and withheld some to protect survivors and ongoing investigations, while lawmakers criticized redactions as potentially shielding powerful individuals[1]. The official DOJ Epstein Library site was last updated on March 7, 2026, indicating ongoing adjustments, though no new major releases or subpoenas involving Pam Bondi, House Oversight, Thomas Massie, Ro Khanna, or FBI 302s were reported[4]. A prior February 1, 2026 press release confirmed publication of 3.5 million pages in compliance with the Epstein Files Transparency Act, including videos and images from multiple investigations[2].

convergence_note: 6 nodes active simultaneously — within the 7-day convergence window defined by the thermostat model.
